channel.title: PublicSafetyFactsHawaii
channel.description: Records-based community education on Hawaii firefighter pay, overtime, and labor rights.

item.title: The Slowest Step Ladder in the Nation
item.description: Hawaii's fire departments climb the slowest step ladder in the nation. A base-rank firefighter takes 28 years to reach the top of the pay scale, the longest of the 37 U.S. departments that publish a conventional step ladder, and grows base pay slower per year than any of them.

item.title: Behind Every Siren
item.description: When the engine rolls out, you see the firefighters. You never see the account clerks, the accountant, the personnel staff, and the secretaries who keep the department running. They are some of its lowest-paid workers, in one of the most expensive places in America to live.

item.title: The Cost-of-Living Gap: What a Firefighter's Paycheck Actually Buys in Hawaii
item.description: Hawaii is the most expensive place in the country to live, and firefighter wages have not kept pace with it. Here is what the numbers look like against rent, groceries, and a median home, in plain dollars.

item.title: Where Maui's Firefighter Vacancies Are, According to the County's Own Roster
item.description: The county's May 2026 staffing roster names ten open firefighter positions, including one on the Lahaina ladder company and two in Wailea. The current vacancy rate is low. The reason that should worry residents is what happens next.

item.title: What the Fire Commission Was Told About Firefighter Pay, and What the Records Show
item.description: In a public meeting, Maui's oversight commission was told that firefighters rank "fairly good to great" on pay. Months later, the county's own arbitration award, budget, and compensation study filled in a fuller picture. Both are in the official minutes.

item.title: Why 5-Person Fire Crews Are the Standard
item.description: The federal research on fire crew size is consistent: five firefighters is the point where every fireground task hits full efficiency and injuries bottom out. Here is the data from NIST, the U.S. Fire Administration, and the Dallas Fire Department, laid out plainly.

item.title: Maui County's Own Study Made the Case for Firefighter Raises
item.description: In March 2025, Maui County commissioned a professional compensation study for its directors. Every argument the consultants used to justify those raises applies equally, in most cases more forcefully, to the county's BU-11 firefighters.

item.title: The Federal Exemption That Costs Hawaii Firefighters Millions
item.description: Hawaii's firefighters can work 53-hour weeks without earning a single dollar of overtime. The law that makes this possible is optional. And most people have never heard of it.

item.title: The Family Behind the Badge
item.description: For every firefighter on a 24-hour shift, someone at home is running the household alone. They never get a title, a line item, or a place in a contract. They are not behind the scenes. They are the story.

item.title: Fewer People Are Applying to Be Firefighters on Maui
item.description: For years, the argument against paying firefighters more was that everyone wants the job. Maui's own entrance-exam data shows the opposite: applications have fallen roughly 73 percent, and the passing scores are dropping with them.

item.title: The Distance Between Recognition and Compensation
item.description: On International Firefighters' Day, counties across Hawaii issued proclamations honoring their firefighters. In the same year, they finalized a below-inflation contract. Here is the difference between recognition and compensation.

item.title: Hawaii's Two-Tier Public Safety System
item.description: Police officers just won the largest pay increase in nearly two decades. Hawaii's firefighters received less than half that - with no step raises for four years. Both outcomes are now final. Here's what the numbers actually say.
